data.title: Snowflake: This Company Isn't SaaS, Calmly 'Buy' As Others Panic Sell
data.description:
If there's one trait as an investor that I think is more important than any other, it's the ability and willingness to change our minds as situations move. I'm not permanently bullish or permanently bearish on any stock. It all depends on where the stock is trading today and how its fundamentals are performing relative to that valuation.

I was deeply bearish on Snowflake Inc. (SNOW), the cloud data warehousing company, as recently as last year, when the stock was flirting with levels just shy of $300. Today, the SaaSpocalypse has put a massive dent into this stock, bringing it down ~40% since the start of the year and wiping off tens of billions in market value. The key question now is, when will the pain end?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Snowflake in December, when the stock had first cratered to the low $200s. I'll admit that I wasn't predicting the so-called "SaaSpocalypse" and that I pulled the "Buy" trigger too early. At the same time, when a stock nosedives this deeply, it's more than appropriate to take a fresh look, and I see no red flags that warrant the massive erosion in Snowflake's multiples. In the span of just a few quarters, Snowflake has made the tremendous journey from significantly overvalued to visibly undervalued, leading me to reiterate my "Buy" rating on the company.

It's impossible to discuss Snowflake without discussing the notion of the SaaSpocalypse itself. To put it into a nutshell, I do believe AI will have an outsized impact on the software sector, but the way in which it will impact incumbent players depends heavily on the type of software and the pricing model.

Complex, data-heavy, and mission-critical software platforms that run daily company operations are unlikely to be ripped out. As are software companies that serve large corporations, particularly those in regulated industries. Where AI will have the biggest impact is indirectly through layoffs. Oracle (ORCL) made waves by laying off ~30,000 employees; Meta recently announced plans to remove ~20% of its staffers, and Block has already begun slicing down its headcount by 40%. This heavily impacts software companies whose business models rest on a per-head seat count. Previously, the market had thought these business models to have a deep moat, with companies unlikely to terminate seats.

From the beginning, Snowflake has insisted that it is not a SaaS company. The company no longer includes this slide in its quarterly investor presentations, but as recently as the first quarter of FY23, the company clarified that its business doesn't rely on seat counts but is entirely dependent on customers' data consumption.

The same is true today. The slide below, contained in the company's most recent investor presentation, showcases its billing model today. Snowflake bills its customers based on compute, storage, and data transfer. It bills upfront, while revenue is recognized as customers actually use the Snowflake platform on a day-to-day basis.

From a practical standpoint, what this means is that Snowflake will be largely unaffected by mass layoffs (like at Oracle and Meta), provided that customers still use the same amount of data stored on Snowflake platforms. In fact, the huge data center capex boom and the fact that AI is unleashing a brand new wave of startups only point to data consumption increasing rather than decreasing.

In my view, Snowflake's selloff is a mere misunderstanding by the market of how AI will impact different software companies, and Snowflake's non-SaaS, consumption-based business model shields it from downside. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

My assertion that the "SaaSpocalypse" won't cause severe harm to Snowflake's business isn't just conjecture, but it's readily observed in Snowflake's most recent data. Take a look at the Q4 earnings summary below:

Snowflake's revenue grew 30% y/y to $1.28 billion, outstripping Wall Street's expectations of $1.25 billion (+27% y/y) by a three-point margin. We note as well that revenue growth accelerated slightly from 29% y/y growth in Q3.

This is just one of several indicators that AI isn't acting as a net headwind to Snowflake. We find as well that the company's net revenue retention rates remained steady at 125%. This indicates that the average Snowflake customer is boosting its spending on the Snowflake platform by 25%. This helps to justify the notion that companies' desire to deploy new applications, particularly buoyed by AI, relies on reams of data, which is precisely what drives Snowflake's business model.

Also, Snowflake is signing a greater chunk of long-term backlog. Its overall remaining performance obligations (RPO, which is essentially backlog) grew 42% y/y to $9.77 billion, far outstripping revenue growth. We note that Snowflake guided to $5.7 billion in product revenue for FY27, so the company's current backlog indicates that it has just shy of two years of revenue already contractually committed.

Again, if customers were looking to decommission Snowflake, replace it with AI solutions, or slice down their spending on the platform, we wouldn't be observing such a large increase in contractual backlog.

Speaking to large deals on the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call, CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy commented as follows:

Bookings and multiyear contracts are a clear indication of the trust that our partners have in their future with Snowflake. And yes, the product acceleration and velocity goes a lot towards convincing customers that we are a platform for the future. We didn't do anything particularly special in the quarters. Yes, we did adjust the compensation plan to also take bookings into account last year. But in many ways, that represents a reversion back to how things were 2 years ago. And we plan to continue that this year. So it's very much business as usual. I do think that the $400 million, $400-plus million deal that we signed is an indication of the importance that we deliver to that large financial services customers. We have previously talked about deals in the $250 million range. I think it represents a maturity of Snowflake as a durable provider, not just today of data services, but also into the future.

The company also continues to scale profitably, with pro forma operating margins of 11%, improving 2 points y/y and still positioning Snowflake as a "Rule of 40" software company.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

At current share prices near $134, Snowflake's market cap has fallen to $46.30 billion. Netting off the substantial $4.78 billion of cash against $2.28 billion of debt (a $2.50 billion net cash position) on Snowflake's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $43.80 billion.

For FY27 (the year for Snowflake ending in January 2027), Snowflake has guided to 27% y/y revenue growth to $5.66 billion in product revenue. Consensus is calling for $5.92 billion in total revenue (including professional services), or 26% y/y growth. The company's expectation of a 23% FCF margin also implies ~$1.36 billion in free cash flow against this revenue profile.

Snowflake trades at:

  • 7.4x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 32.2x EV/FY27 FCF

Though not a bargain-basement stock per se, we note that Snowflake trades reasonably for a "Rule of 40" software stock that is enjoying consumption-based AI tailwinds, as evidenced by the recent acceleration in growth rates plus the buildup in long-term backlog. Also of note is the fact that Snowflake is expecting to reduce stock-based compensation as a percentage of revenue to 27% in FY27, from 34% in FY26 and 41% in FY25 - addressing a key concern many investors have about dilution with Snowflake and other software companies (with share prices declining much deeper after Snowflake's earnings release, we think stock compensation levels could fall even further this year).

Of course, there are risks here to keep in mind. The macro remains unstable, and across the software sector, many companies are reporting slower deal cycles. And amid foundational AI companies striking large deals with cloud providers like Oracle for data center capacity, we could see heightened competition for data warehousing against larger players.

That said, I think Snowflake's huge slide from peaks gives us a de-risked entry point into this stock. Take advantage of this dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Even After Recent Gains, The Valuation Is Still Compelling For Lake Shore Bancorp
data.description:
Lake Shore Bancorp (LSBK) is a perfect example of the kind of small-cap regional bank that I enjoyed researching last year. With a market cap of just $113.1 million, less than $1 billion in total assets, and no Wall Street analyst coverage, there is little reason for anyone outside of the company's home state of New York to know much about its operations. However, its stock has gained over 53% in the last 12 months and still trades well below its book value. These gains greatly outpace those from the S&P 500 as well as the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE).

I first wrote about Lake Shore on September 11, and its stock has produced a total return of 21.4% in just seven months of time. I rated LSBK as a Buy at that time based on its strong financials, attractive valuation, and potential upside as it entered its first full-year following its mutual-to-stock conversion. Based on its strong finish to 2025 and the prospects for the year ahead, I do not see a reason to change my opinion on the company at this time. Below, I will discuss my reasons for continued optimism for Lake Shore and give a brief preview of its Q1 2026 earnings, which are due later this month.

Company Overview

Lake Shore Bancorp is the holding company for Lake Shore Bank, which is headquartered in Dunkirk, New York. LSBK operates 10 bank branch locations in Western New York. Four are in Chautauqua County and six in Erie County, in the Buffalo-Niagara Falls metro area. Lake Shore Bank offers traditional consumer and commercial banking services, including commercial real estate lending, residential mortgage lending, and treasury management.

Last year was a busy one for Lake Shore Bancorp as it undertook the process of a mutual-to-stock conversion. On May 23, the company announced a common stock offering as part of the conversion process. The new company began its first day as a stockholding company on Monday, July 21, with approximately 7.8 million shares of common stock outstanding.

When I last wrote about the company, it was somewhat challenging to obtain all of the information that I normally want to read through before making a complete analysis. I have a clearer picture of the bank’s operations since the company filed its 2025 10-K on March 17. Lake Shore ended last year with total assets of $727.3 million, a 6.1% increase from the end of 2024. The bank’s loan portfolio, after allowance for credit losses, was valued at $555 million at the end of 2025, a modest 2.0% expansion over the previous 12 months.

A more complete breakdown of the loans outstanding for the bank is given below. Commercial real estate loans hold the largest allocation at $327 million, or 58.7% of the total gross loans. Residential mortgages are a distant second at $150 million, or 26.9%. Home equity loans were the third most common at just $47 million.

Total deposits at the bank grew only fractionally in 2025 to finish at $573 million. Only 16.7%, or $96 million, of those deposits are held in non-interest-bearing accounts. However, the bank also controls an additional $114 million in savings accounts or interest-bearing demand accounts that paid less than 0.1% interest on average in 2025, helping to keep the bank’s overall cost of deposits below the national average for banks with less than $1 billion in total assets.

LSBK pays a regular quarterly dividend of $0.09. This gives the stock a forward yield of 2.31%, about 60 basis points below the median for the financial sector. The payout ratio for the company is currently just below average, which could imply capacity for a dividend increase in the future.

Analysis of Lake Shore Bancorp

Lake Shore Bancorp ended 2025 with a great deal of positive momentum. The company announced record earnings for the full year. In Q4 2025, LSBK reported net income of $1.9 million, or $0.26 per diluted share. That quarterly EPS number was 36.8% better than Q4 2024. For FY 2025, Lake Shore netted $7.3 million, or $0.97 per diluted share, compared to $4.9 million, or $0.65 per share, during the previous 12-month period. For the full year, the EPS improvement equaled 49.2%.

Kim Liddel, Lake Shore President and CEO, made the following statement as part of the earnings announcement:

Our fourth quarter and 2025 year-end results reflect the effectiveness of our strategies. We increased core earnings, improved margins, strengthened the balance sheet, reduced borrowings and non-performing assets, and grew tangible book value. Our disciplined focus on prudent loan growth and core deposits positions us well for 2026 and delivering shareholder value.

Given its earnings for the past 12 months, LSBK trades at a trailing PE of almost 16. This is certainly not bargain territory for a community bank. However, my optimism for the company is based on the improvements that were made in 2025 and how much more room is left to go in 2026. Consider the statistics below.

  • Net interest margin rose 13 basis points from Q3 2025 and 52 basis points over the full year to end Q4 2025 at 3.85%.
  • The bank’s efficiency ratio improved 12.79% during the year to end 2025 at 69.21%. This is just 2.32% above the national average for banks of its size.
  • LSBK was able to reduce its dependence on wholesale funding by repaying all outstanding borrowings in FY 2025.
  • Nonperforming assets for the bank declined 32 basis points to end the year at just 0.23%.
  • The bank ended the year in an outstanding capital position with Tier 1 Leverage at 16.65% and Total Risk-Based Capital of 23.51%.

Perhaps the most telling statistic from the company’s 2025 performance was the improvement made in its book value. LSBK started the year with a tangible book value of $11.57 per share. It ended the year at $18.10. That 56.4% improvement is the largest I can remember in a one-year period for a regional bank. A big part of the TBV improvement is related to the capital that was raised in the middle of the year.

At its current share price of $15.62, the stock trades at just 0.86 times its TBV. That is a valuation most often found in low-performing banks with asset quality issues. As a matter of fact, if Lake Shore met the $3 billion asset minimum, it would be one of the 10 cheapest banks in the US based on its price-to-TBV ratio in the most recent rankings from S&P Global.

There are other aspects of the bank’s operations that do still leave room for improvement in 2026. The company reported a return on average assets of 1.04% at the end of Q4 2025. That was a 19-basis point improvement from Q4 2024, but it was still about 17 basis points behind the average for its peers. For FY 2025, LSBK’s return on equity was only 6.41%. This was 79 basis points ahead of FY 2024 but well below the 10% or better that I usually find in the banks I recommend on Seeking Alpha. The bank’s low leverage is impacting this number, but greater NIM expansion, higher net interest income, and a lower efficiency ratio would all help normalize this metric in 2026.

Looking ahead to what Q1 2026 may bring for the bank, I expect the company to easily beat the $0.14 EPS that was reported for Q1 2025. Of course, with no analyst coverage, there are no earnings expectations to beat (or miss). LSBK reported earnings of $0.32 per share in Q3 2025 but stepped back to just $0.26 in Q4. The biggest culprit for this decline was a $228,000 decrease in earnings on bank-owned life insurance, which came about after a death benefit was paid in Q3. This leads me to believe that something in the range of $0.24 to $0.28 is reasonable to expect, barring any other additional charges. One of the challenges for investing in small-cap banks is that these small charges, like the one mentioned here for life insurance, are more meaningful to the company’s overall bottom line.

Risks to Consider

While I have referred to Lake Shore as a small-cap bank earlier in my analysis, it is really a micro-cap bank that carries additional risk with any investment. With a loan portfolio valued at only $555 million, it would not take much for the nonperforming ratios from the bank to increase significantly. The bank’s commercial real estate loans are primarily connected to properties in Western New York. CRE payments are dependent on the health of the businesses that reside inside their properties. CRE loans typically involve larger loan balances concentrated with single borrowers or related borrowers that may be impacted by the same economic headwinds. In the case of any banking crisis, like the one experienced in 2023, small-cap banks would likely be more negatively impacted as investors rushed to the security offered by larger institutions.

Another risk to consider before investing in LSBK is the bank’s limited geographic footprint. The company is heavily dependent on a healthy economy in Buffalo and the surrounding region. A recent report from Zillow gave reason for some optimism as it named Buffalo as the most affordable housing market in the US. Almost 71% of all home listings in the area are considered “affordable,” with a typical home value just under $272,000. This could be welcome news for a city that has seen below-average population growth over the last several years. Trade with Canada also plays a significant role in the economy of Buffalo, but Canadian trade and tariffs have been more complicated than usual over the last year, impacting the businesses on the US side of the border.

LSBK is also a relatively thinly traded stock. Over the last three months, its average daily trading volume is under 10,000 shares. This lack of activity can widen the gap between bid-ask spreads. Limit orders are suggested when making a purchase in Lake Shore Bancorp. Should a position need to be sold quickly at a market price, the ensuing sale may push the price down lower than the previous transaction.

Conclusion

In spite of its depressed valuation, an investment in Lake Shore Bancorp still comes with a great deal of risk. The company does not have a long track record of public results to parse through and exists largely unnoticed by most of the investment community. What I have uncovered in my research leads me to believe that LSBK is still worthy of a Buy rating heading into its Q1 2026 earnings report later this month.

data.title: CIO Notebook: Payrolls Surprise As Fed Mulls Next Move
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

We remain positive on risk assets and are poised to take advantage of both what we believe are mispricings in parts of the bond markets as well as more attractive valuations within equities following March’s market weakness.

March non-farm payrolls were meaningfully higher than consensus even as the conflict in the Middle East wears on. Payrolls increased by +178k in the month ahead of expectations for a +59k print. Payrolls were revised lower for February to -133k from -92k while January was revised up modestly by +7k. In aggregate, today’s print brings the trailing three-month average payroll gains to +68k – notably stronger than the second half of 2025.

Driving the print, once again, was health care. While declining by -28k in February due to a large strike at Kaiser Permanente, health care job growth rebounded in March, with the sector adding +76k. Leisure and hospitality was also notably higher (+44k) as was social assistance (+14k). Notably, construction (+26k), transportation and warehousing (+21k), and manufacturing (+15k) were all positive in March as well; these green shoots are consistent with most recent release of ISM Manufacturing PMI of 52.7, the measure’s third straight increase after 10 months of contraction. Areas of weakness in the report were financial activities (-15k) and the Federal government, which shed another -18k jobs to bring the cumulative decline in U.S. government jobs to almost -12% since October 2024.

The unemployment rate ticked down to 4.3% on a further decline in the participation rate, which fell from 62.0% to 61.9% – the lowest level since November 2021. Employed persons were down by -64k, and there were 332k fewer unemployed persons as many of these individuals left the workforce. Average hourly earnings decelerated as well, up only +0.2% month-over-month and +3.5% year-over-year, while average hours worked declined from 34.3 to 34.2.

Our perspective is that this print, combined with the sharp decline in February, does little to meaningfully impact the Fed’s path in the short term. Already uneasy about the one-time (but admittedly rolling) transmission of tariffs to prices, and mindful of the risk for the translation of near-term higher energy prices to other end goods and services (the shift from transitory to structural), the Fed is likely on hold for at least the next two meetings. Another meaningfully negative print this month may have catalyzed the Fed to act sooner and/or more emphatically, but save evidence of broad-based deterioration, the inflation side of the dual mandate is weighing heavily. There is also little evidence of a material deceleration in economic activity, particularly when considering the pace of jobless claims, thus providing a margin of safety for the Fed as it monitors the impacts of the Middle East conflict.

Although we acknowledge the near-term challenges presented by higher energy prices, we believe that rates have likely moved too much in terms of pricing out cuts in the U.S. and pricing in several hikes in Europe. The duration of the Iranian conflict is key – oil prices over $100 a barrel for an extended period of time will likely yield demand destruction, which would slow the global economy and point the central bank compass back towards accommodation. Based on the political backdrop in the U.S. and the growing discontent of voters centered around affordability concerns, as well as the Trump Administration’s comments about looking for an off-ramp, we believe oil prices will fall at least modestly over the next two to three weeks. Even if a portion of the current “war premium” remains in the price, the levels at which oil and natural gas would trade in a de-escalation scenario would be digestible. Therefore, we remain positive on risk assets and are poised to take advantage of both what we believe are mispricings in parts of the bond markets, as well as more attractive valuations within equities following March’s market weakness.

Important Information:

This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types. Diversification does not guarantee profit or protect against loss in declining markets. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Investments in private equity are speculative and involve a higher degree of risk than more traditional investments. Investments in private equity are intended for sophisticated investors only. Unless otherwise indicated, returns shown reflect reinvestment of dividends and distributions. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Portfolio positioning views expressed herein are those of Neuberger Berman’s Private Wealth Investment Group, which may include those of the Neuberger Berman’s Asset Allocation Committee. Asset allocation and positioning views are based on a hypothetical reference portfolio. The Private Wealth Investment Group analyzes market and economic indicators to develop asset allocation strategies. The Private Wealth Investment Group works in partnership with the Office of the CIO. The Private Wealth Investment Group also consults regularly with portfolio managers and investment officers across the firm. The Asset Allocation Committee is comprised of professionals across multiple disciplines, including equity and fixed income strategists and portfolio managers. The Asset Allocation Committee reviews and sets long-term asset allocation models, establishes preferred near-term tactical asset class allocations and, upon request, reviews asset allocations for large, diversified mandates.
